title: Flick me once and I know it’s you! Flicking-based Implicit Authentication for Smartwatch
venue: 2023 International Conference on Computing, Networking and Communications (ICNC): Communications and Information Security Symposium

method.training_strategy: Grid search coupled with 6-fold cross-validation on training sets (48 samples per user)

evaluation.context: Achieved in sitting scenario for majority of users
Achieved in sitting scenario (reported as 100% TPR) for majority of users

evaluation_protocol: 48/12 split for training and testing per user; other users used as imposters for FAR calculation

reported_limitations: Standing scenario shows worse performance than sitting due to added variability and lack of stability

key_contribution: Proposed FlickAuth, an implicit authentication scheme leveraging common wrist flicking movements
Developed a flick motion extraction algorithm to isolate movement from noise
Identified a reduced set of 10 high-impact features for efficient on-device classification
main_strength: On-device unsupervised training removes the need for cloud-based imposter data
main_weakness: Performance sensitivity to user posture (sitting vs standing)
insights.notes_for_review: The paper demonstrates a proof-of-concept for behavioral authentication via flicking, but requires significantly larger and more diverse datasets to validate real-world applicability.
